title: SQL Injection in Login
description: User-supplied email and password are interpolated directly into a raw SQL query string without parameterization. An attacker can supply a payload such as `admin' OR '1'='1` to bypass authentication entirely. The query is executed via `models.sequelize.query()` with no bound parameters.

title: SQL Injection in Product Search
description: The user-controlled `criteria` parameter is interpolated directly into a LIKE clause in a raw Sequelize query. An attacker can inject UNION SELECT statements to exfiltrate any table in the database, including the Users table with password hashes. Input is only truncated to 200 characters, not sanitized.

title: NoSQL Injection via MongoDB $where in Order Tracking
description: A user-supplied order ID is concatenated into a MongoDB `$where` JavaScript expression that is executed server-side. The `$where` operator runs arbitrary JavaScript, allowing an attacker to craft payloads like `'; return true; //` to access all records or perform server-side code execution. This bypasses all query-level access controls.

title: NoSQL Injection via MongoDB $where in Product Reviews
description: The product `id` from the request URL is concatenated directly into a `$where` clause: `'this.product == ' + id`. An attacker can inject JavaScript that executes in the MongoDB context, enabling data exfiltration across all reviews or denial of service via expensive operations.

title: Server-Side Template Injection / Code Injection via eval()
description: Usernames matching the pattern `#{...}` have their inner content extracted and passed directly to `eval()`. An attacker who sets their username to `#{process.mainModule.require('child_process').execSync('id')}` can achieve arbitrary remote code execution with the application's privileges. This is a textbook SSTI escalated to full RCE.

title: Use of eval() for CAPTCHA Calculation
description: Mathematical CAPTCHA expressions are evaluated using `eval()`. While the expression is constructed server-side, relying on `eval()` is inherently dangerous and any future change to expression construction could introduce code injection. The use of `eval()` should be replaced with a safe math parser.

title: XXE via Unsafe XML Parsing with noent:true
description: Uploaded XML files are parsed with `libxml.parseXml()` using the `noent: true` option, which enables XML external entity expansion. An attacker can upload an XML file with an external entity referencing `/etc/passwd` or an internal network endpoint to read arbitrary local files or perform SSRF. The billion-laughs DoS attack is also enabled.

title: Unsafe YAML Deserialization
description: Uploaded YAML files are processed with `yaml.load()`, which can instantiate arbitrary JavaScript objects. This allows YAML bomb attacks (recursive anchors causing exponential memory/CPU consumption) and may enable prototype pollution or object injection depending on how the parsed result is used downstream.

title: SSRF via User-Controlled Profile Image URL
description: The `imageUrl` field from the request body is passed directly to `fetch()` without allowlist validation. An attacker can supply URLs targeting internal services (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` for cloud metadata, or `http://localhost:6379` for Redis), enabling internal network reconnaissance and credential theft.

title: Path Traversal in ZIP Complaint Upload
description: Zip file entries are extracted using paths constructed as `'uploads/complaints/' + fileName` where `fileName` comes from the archive. The path traversal check using `absolutePath.includes(path.resolve('.'))` is insufficient because a path like `uploads/complaints/../../secret.txt` still contains the base directory string. An attacker can overwrite arbitrary files outside the uploads directory.

title: Path Traversal in Log File Server
description: The `file` query parameter is used directly in `res.sendFile(path.resolve('logs/', file))`. The only check is whether the filename contains a `/`, but URL-encoded traversal sequences (`%2F`, `..%2F`) or OS-specific variants may bypass this filter. An attacker could read application source, configuration files, or private keys.

title: IDOR in Data Export Endpoint
description: The data export endpoint uses `req.body.UserId` directly when querying memories, orders, and reviews, with no check that the supplied UserId matches the authenticated user's ID. Any authenticated user can supply another user's ID to export their complete data including memories, orders, and reviews.

title: Missing Authentication on All-Orders Endpoint
description: The `allOrders()` route handler queries the entire orders collection without requiring any authentication middleware. Any unauthenticated client can retrieve the full order history for all customers, exposing PII including names, addresses, and order details.

title: CAPTCHA Bypass via Missing Entry Logic
description: The CAPTCHA verification condition is `if (!captchas[0] || req.body.answer === captchas[0].answer)`, which calls `next()` when no CAPTCHA record is found rather than rejecting the request. An attacker can trigger a state where no CAPTCHA exists (e.g., by race condition or session manipulation) to bypass CAPTCHA verification entirely.

title: MD5 Used for Password Hashing
description: Passwords are hashed using MD5 (`crypto.createHash('md5')`), which is a fast, broken cryptographic hash with no salt. MD5 password hashes can be cracked in seconds using rainbow tables or GPU brute force. Any credential database compromise leads to near-instant password recovery for all users.

title: Hardcoded RSA Private Key in Source Code
description: A private RSA key is embedded directly in the source code and committed to the repository. Anyone with read access to the codebase can extract this key and use it to sign arbitrary JWTs, impersonating any user including administrators. Version control history preserves the key even if later removed.

title: Hardcoded HMAC Secret Key
description: The HMAC secret `pa4qacea4VK9t9nGv7yZtwmj` is hardcoded in source. This secret is used for security-sensitive HMAC operations throughout the application. An attacker who reads the source can forge any HMAC-protected value, bypassing integrity checks.

title: Private Encryption Keys Stored in Repository
description: The `encryptionkeys/` directory contains private cryptographic keys checked into version control. These keys are permanently embedded in git history even if the files are later deleted. Any party with repository access—including contributors, CI systems, or compromised hosting—obtains the private keys.

title: Ancient jsonwebtoken Library with Algorithm Confusion Vulnerability
description: The application uses `jsonwebtoken` version `0.4.0` from 2014, which does not validate the `alg` header in JWTs. An attacker can craft a token using the `none` algorithm or switch from RS256 to HS256 using the public key as the secret, achieving authentication bypass. This is a well-known and documented critical vulnerability.

title: Outdated express-jwt with Known Vulnerabilities
description: The application pins `express-jwt` to version `0.1.3`, a 2014 release. This version contains documented security vulnerabilities including improper algorithm verification and token validation flaws. The library has been extensively patched in subsequent releases.

title: Outdated sanitize-html with Bypass Vulnerabilities
description: Version `1.4.2` of `sanitize-html` is pinned, which contains known XSS filter bypass vulnerabilities fixed in later versions. Relying on a known-broken sanitization library means XSS protections throughout the application may be circumvented with documented bypass payloads.

title: Open Redirect via Substring URL Matching
description: The redirect allowlist check uses `url.includes(allowedUrl)` rather than exact hostname matching. An attacker can craft a URL such as `https://attacker.com/?ref=https://allowlisted-domain.com` that passes the check while redirecting to an external malicious site. This enables phishing attacks using the application's trusted domain.

title: Hardcoded Hashids Salts Expose ID Encoding
description: Hashids are initialized with plaintext salts such as `'this is my salt'` hardcoded in source. Hashids are not a security primitive but are sometimes used to obscure IDs; with known salts, an attacker can predict or forge encoded challenge continuation codes, potentially skipping challenge completion validation.

title: Sensitive File Content Reflected in Error Messages
description: When XML parsing fails, up to 400 characters of the raw uploaded XML content are included in the error message passed to `next()`. If the XML contained data extracted via XXE (e.g., contents of `/etc/passwd`), that data is included in the error response and potentially logged, compounding the XXE information disclosure.

title: Missing CSRF Protection on State-Changing Endpoints
description: Profile update and other state-changing POST endpoints do not implement CSRF token validation. The application only checks for a specific referer header from `htmledit.squarefree.com` rather than using a proper synchronized token pattern. Attackers can host malicious pages that silently submit forms to change victim users' profile data.
